# Financial Deep Research: Texas Instruments (TXN)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07
Stage 1 baseline provided: No — §9 notes the absence.

---

## 1. Recent Financial Performance & Quarterly Trends

### Most Recent Report
Texas Instruments reported first-quarter 2026 results on April 22, 2026. Revenue was $4.83 billion, up 19% year-over-year and 9% sequentially. Operating profit was $1.81 billion (37.5% of revenue), up 37% year-over-year, and net income was $1.55 billion. Diluted earnings per share were $1.68, up 31% year-over-year and comfortably above the consensus estimate of approximately $1.36–$1.38. The EPS figure included a $0.05 benefit from discrete tax items not originally guided. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results and shareholder returns / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

Gross margin expanded to 58.0% in Q1 2026, up 120 basis points from the year-ago quarter and 210 basis points sequentially. Operating margin was 37.5%, up from 32.5% in Q1 2025 and 33.3% in Q4 2025. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

### Most Recent Full Year Report
For the full year 2025, TI generated revenue of $17.68 billion, up 13.0% from $15.64 billion in 2024. Operating profit was $6.02 billion (34.1% of revenue), compared with $5.47 billion (34.9% of revenue) in 2024. Net income was $5.00 billion ($5.45 per diluted share) versus $4.80 billion ($5.20 per diluted share) in 2024. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

The year included $117 million in restructuring charges and other items related to operational efficiency efforts, including planned closures of two remaining 150mm factories and a non-cash goodwill impairment tied to custom ASIC products. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

### Key Business Drivers in Recent Quarters
The cyclical recovery that began in 2024 accelerated through Q1 2026. The industrial end market — TI’s largest revenue contributor — grew more than 30% year-over-year in Q1 2026 and more than 20% sequentially, with broad-based strength across all geographies and sectors. Data center revenue expanded approximately 90% year-over-year and more than 25% sequentially, driven by power delivery and thermal management products. Communications equipment grew roughly 25% year-over-year and more than 30% sequentially. Automotive increased only mid-single digits year-over-year and was flat sequentially, with weakness in China offset by growth in other regions. Personal electronics was flat year-over-year. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

Management has characterized the recovery as continuing “though at a slower pace than prior upturns,” noting that industrial demand remains roughly 15% below its 2022 peak, suggesting runway remains before prior-cycle highs are reclaimed. [Source: Company Q3 earnings call / Title: Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2025-10-22] ***

### Segment Performance
In Q1 2026, Analog revenue was $3.92 billion (81.3% of total), up 22% year-over-year, with operating profit of $1.64 billion (41.7% margin). Embedded Processing revenue was $723 million (15.0% of total), up 12% year-over-year, with operating profit of $122 million (16.9% margin). The Other segment (DLP, calculators, custom ASIC) generated $178 million, down 16% year-over-year, with operating profit of $48 million; this segment now includes recurring acquisition charges related to the pending Silicon Labs deal. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

For the full year 2025, Analog revenue grew 15.2% and Embedded Processing grew 6.5%. Embedded profitability has been pressured by higher manufacturing costs tied to the LFAB (Lehi, Utah) ramp, which disproportionately burdens that segment until loadings increase. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

### Geographic & FX Exposure
TI does not provide quarterly geographic revenue splits in its earnings releases, but the 2025 annual report and management commentary indicate material exposure to China. Revenue from China-headquartered customers has declined materially from a peak of roughly $10.0 billion in 2021 to approximately $3.8 billion in 2025, reflecting both cyclical downturns and geopolitical headwinds. China now represents roughly 20% of revenue, down from prior-cycle levels closer to 40%. [Source: Stock Analysis On / Title: TXN Analysis of Geographic Areas / Date: undated] No tag; corroborated by management commentary on China auto weakness. Management emphasized a “China-for-China” manufacturing and inventory strategy to mitigate tariff and trade-policy risks. [Source: AInvest / Title: Texas Instruments Rises on Strong Earnings, but Tariff Risks Loom Large / Date: 2025-04-24] *

---

## 2. Growth Trajectory

### Trajectory Assessment
TI’s revenue trajectory is recovering from a pronounced cyclical trough. The three-year compound annual revenue growth for 2023–2025 was negative 4.1%, reflecting the depth of the 2023–2024 industrial downturn. However, fiscal 2025 growth of 13.0% and Q1 2026 growth of 19% signal a robust upturn. The Q2 2026 revenue guidance range of $5.0 billion to $5.4 billion implies roughly 21% year-over-year growth at the midpoint and 8% sequential growth — a pace CEO Haviv Ilan described as slightly above seasonal. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

The two-year look-back (Q1 2024 to Q1 2026) shows a V-shaped pattern: revenue troughed around $3.6–$4.1 billion per quarter in early-to-mid 2024 before reaccelerating. Because TI serves long-cycle industrial and automotive markets, a multi-year window is appropriate. The current recovery appears driven by inventory restocking, improving factory automation demand, and data-center buildouts rather than entirely new demand creation. Management has repeatedly noted that customer inventories are at low levels and depletion appears to be behind the industry, which suggests the current growth phase has underlying support from supply-chain normalization. [Source: Company Q3 earnings call / Title: Q3 2025 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2025-10-22] ***

### Peer-Relative Growth Context
TI’s growth is entirely organic, whereas peer medians in the semiconductor sector frequently include acquisition-driven contributions. In the 2025 annual report, TI noted that its 13.0% revenue growth was above the peer median, but its three-year CAGR was below median. The analog and microcontroller peer group (Analog Devices, NXP, ON Semiconductor) has generally seen similar cyclical patterns, though TI’s exposure to industrial markets is heavier than many peers, making its recovery profile more tightly coupled to global manufacturing PMIs and capital expenditure cycles. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

---

## 3. Margin & Profitability Profile

### Margin Metrics and Justification
For a vertically integrated analog and embedded semiconductor manufacturer, gross margin and operating margin are the most meaningful profitability lenses. Gross margin captures manufacturing leverage across TI’s 300mm wafer fabs, while operating margin reflects the company’s disciplined R&D and SG&A control amid a heavy capital-expansion cycle. Free cash flow margin is also critical given the recent capex bulge, but profitability analysis begins at the factory level.

TI’s gross margin was 58.0% in Q1 2026, up from 55.9% in Q4 2025 and 56.8% in Q1 2025, but still below the 10-year median of roughly 63.9% and well off the 2022 peak near 69%. The compression over 2023–2025 reflected underutilization of new 300mm capacity and higher depreciation burdens from the Sherman and Lehi fabs. With factory loadings now improving, incremental gross margin fall-through is guided at 75%–85% (excluding depreciation), suggesting that further revenue growth should drive disproportionate profit expansion. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] *** [Source: GuruFocus / Title: TXN Gross Margin % / Date: 2026-01-16] **

Operating margin was 37.5% in Q1 2026, up sharply from 32.5% in the year-ago quarter. Trailing-twelve-month operating margin as of late 2025 was approximately 32.3%, down from 34.9% in FY2024 and 42.3% in FY2023. [Source: CompaniesMarketCap / Title: TXN Operating Margin / Date: undated] No tag; used cautiously as directional context.

### Peer-Relative Profitability Context
TI’s average operating profit margin for 2023–2025 was 37.0%, which the company states was above the median for its semiconductor peer group. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] *** However, current margins remain depressed relative to TI’s own historical standards and to the peak levels achieved when 300mm utilization was highest. The Embedded Processing segment’s operating margin of 16.9% in Q1 2026 remains a drag relative to Analog’s 41.7%, largely due to the LFAB ramp costs. If LFAB loadings normalize, embedded margins should recover, providing a second leg of operating leverage beyond the Analog recovery.

---

## 4. Cash Generation & Quality

### Cash Generation Quality Assessment
TI defines free cash flow as cash flow from operations less capital expenditures, plus proceeds from CHIPS Act incentives. This non-GAAP measure is central to management’s capital allocation narrative. For the twelve months ended March 31, 2026, free cash flow was $4.35 billion (23.6% of revenue), up from $1.72 billion in the prior-year period. Cash flow from operations over the same trailing-twelve-month period was $7.82 billion. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

In Q1 2026 specifically, operating cash flow was $1.52 billion, capex was $676 million (down from roughly $1.1 billion in Q1 2025), and CHIPS Act proceeds contributed $555 million, yielding quarterly free cash flow of $1.40 billion. This compares with a free cash flow outflow in Q1 2025 when capex was elevated. [Source: Barron's / Title: Texas Instruments Reports Solid Earnings / Date: 2026-04-23] * [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

The quality of cash generation is high: TI’s cash flow from operations consistently exceeds net income over multi-year periods, supported by depreciation on a growing manufacturing asset base. However, the inclusion of CHIPS Act incentives ($965 million in the trailing-twelve-month free cash flow) means reported free cash flow is currently inflated by government funding. Excluding CHIPS proceeds, underlying free cash flow generation is recovering but remains lower than peak-cycle levels. Management has expressed confidence in reaching $8 of free cash flow per share in 2026, up from $4.77 in FY2025, supported by revenue growth, gross margin expansion, and capex moderation. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

### Working Capital & Cash Conversion
Working capital dynamics are material to TI’s cash conversion. Accounts receivable were $2.25 billion at the end of Q1 2026, up from $1.86 billion a year prior, reflecting higher revenue. Days sales outstanding has remained stable at approximately 39–40 days. Inventory was $4.70 billion at quarter-end, down $109 million sequentially, with inventory days declining to 209 from 222 at year-end 2025 and 241 at year-end 2024. Management has stated that inventory days are expected to drift lower as the upcycle progresses. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] *** [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

The company’s internal inventory strategy aims to maintain high finished-goods availability to support short lead times. While inventory dollars remain elevated historically, the composition has shifted toward work-in-process ($2.36 billion) and finished goods ($1.88 billion), suggesting deliberate positioning for demand rather than obsolescence risk. Accounts payable declined to $638 million in Q1 2026 from $866 million a year ago, which may reflect timing of supplier payments. Overall, cash conversion appears healthy and improving as revenue growth outpaces inventory accumulation.

### Earnings Quality
Earnings quality in Q1 2026 was generally sound. The $0.05 EPS benefit from discrete tax items was modest relative to the $1.68 headline. Acquisition charges of $17 million related to the Silicon Labs deal were booked in the “Other” segment; management expects these to recur quarterly at a similar run rate until the deal closes in the first half of 2027, after which charges will spike and then normalize. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] *** There were no material non-cash gains or restructuring items in Q1 2026, though FY2025 included the aforementioned $117 million restructuring charge and goodwill impairment.

---

## 5. Balance Sheet & Liquidity

TI’s balance sheet remains investment-grade. At March 31, 2026, total cash and short-term investments were $5.1 billion ($3.55 billion cash plus $1.55 billion short-term investments). Total debt outstanding was $14.0 billion with a weighted average coupon of approximately 4.0%. Stockholders’ equity was $16.78 billion against total liabilities of $17.62 billion, yielding a debt-to-total-capital ratio of roughly 45.6%. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] *** [Source: MarketBeat / Title: TXN Financials 2026 / Date: 2026-04-28] **

### Debt Maturity Profile & Refinancing Risk
The debt maturity profile, as disclosed in the 2025 annual report, is long-dated and laddered. Near-term maturities include $500 million of 1.125% notes due in 2026 and $1.15 billion in current-portion long-term debt at Q1 2026. Larger tranches include $650 million due 2027, $700 million due 2028, $1.4 billion due 2029–2030, and multiple billion-dollar tranches extending to 2063. The longest-dated bonds ($1.55 billion due 2063 at 5.05%, $750 million due 2054 at 5.15%) were issued during the low-rate environment of 2023–2025 and lock in historically attractive coupons. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

Refinancing risk appears low. TI has no commercial paper outstanding and an undrawn revolving credit facility. Interest and debt expense was $141 million in Q1 2026 ($543 million for FY2025), comfortably covered by operating profit. With a weighted average coupon near 4% and substantial cash generation, the company can service and refinance obligations without strain. The primary balance sheet risk is the planned $7.5 billion all-cash acquisition of Silicon Labs, which TI intends to fund with cash on hand plus debt financing arranged ahead of closing. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] *** [Source: EE News Europe / Title: TI Silicon Labs acquisition / Date: 2026-02-06] *

### Assessment
Liquidity is adequate but not excessive relative to the pending acquisition. The $5.1 billion cash position will not alone cover the $7.5 billion purchase price, implying additional debt issuance before closing in the first half of 2027. Pro forma leverage will rise, though the company’s cash-generative model and planned $450 million in annual manufacturing/operational synergies within three years should support deleveraging. [Source: EE News Europe / Title: TI Silicon Labs acquisition / Date: 2026-02-06] *

---

## 6. Capital Deployment & Returns on Capital

### Share Repurchases
In Q1 2026, TI repurchased $158 million of its stock, down from $653 million in Q1 2025. Over the trailing twelve months, repurchases totaled $982 million, down from $1.58 billion in the prior-year period. For the full year 2025, TI repurchased 8.5 million shares for $1.48 billion. The company maintains a substantial authorization (an additional $15 billion was authorized in September 2022), but buyback activity has slowed as management has prioritized dividends and balance-sheet flexibility ahead of the Silicon Labs closing. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] *** [Source: Company proxy / Title: 2026 Proxy Statement / Date: 2026-03-03] ***

### Dividends
TI is a high-dividend-yield stock in the semiconductor sector. The quarterly dividend was raised to $1.42 per share in Q1 2026, up from $1.36 a year prior, marking 22 consecutive years of dividend increases. Q1 2026 dividends paid totaled $1.29 billion. Over the trailing twelve months, dividends accounted for $5.05 billion of the $6.03 billion returned to shareholders. At the current rate, the annual dividend is $5.68 per share. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] *** [Source: DividendMax / Title: TXN Dividends / Date: 2026-01-15] *

### M&A / Capex
Capital expenditures were $676 million in Q1 2026 and $4.1 billion on a trailing-twelve-month basis, down from a peak of $4.8 billion in FY2024. Management has guided 2026 capex to $2.0 billion–$3.0 billion, marking the end of a six-year elevated investment cycle. Beyond 2026, capex will be tied to revenue growth expectations. The manufacturing buildout includes seven U.S. fabs across Sherman (TX), Richardson (TX), and Lehi (UT), representing a total planned investment of more than $60 billion. TI has received direct CHIPS Act funding and investment tax credits; Q1 2026 included $555 million in direct funding tied to Sherman fab milestones. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] *** [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

The pending $7.5 billion acquisition of Silicon Labs is the dominant M&A event. Expected to close in the first half of 2027, the deal adds wireless connectivity (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, Zigbee, Thread) to TI’s embedded portfolio. TI plans to reshore Silicon Labs’ production from external foundries into its internal 300mm and assembly/test footprint. [Source: EE News Europe / Title: TI Silicon Labs acquisition / Date: 2026-02-06] *

### Returns on Capital
TI’s return on invested capital (ROIC) remains above its cost of capital, though it has compressed through the downturn. GuruFocus data calculates TTM ROIC at approximately 19.9% as of early 2026, with an estimated WACC near 10.5%. Annualized ROIC in Q3 2025 was 21.7%. [Source: GuruFocus / Title: TXN ROIC % / Date: 2026-01-16] ** For FY2024, ROIC was approximately 19.2%. The spread remains positive, indicating value creation, but the spread has narrowed from peak-cycle levels above 30% due to the large invested-capital base built during the fab expansion. If revenue continues to recover and capex moderates, ROIC should expand as incremental revenue falls through at high margins on a now-largely-depreciated asset base.

---

## 7. Management Guidance & Forward Outlook

### Most Recent Full Year Report Guidance
TI does not issue full-year revenue or earnings guidance. In the 2025 annual report, management stated that beyond 2026, capital expenditures will be dependent on revenue and growth expectations, and that the company expects to continue benefiting from CHIPS Act incentives including a 35% investment tax credit on qualifying manufacturing investments for assets placed in service after December 31, 2025, and direct funding of up to $1.6 billion for three large-scale 300mm wafer fabs. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

### Most Recent Report Guidance
For the second quarter of 2026, TI guided revenue between $5.00 billion and $5.40 billion and EPS between $1.77 and $2.05. The effective tax rate for Q2 is expected to be approximately 13%. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] *** The revenue midpoint of $5.20 billion implies roughly 8% sequential growth and approximately 21% year-over-year growth.

Capital expenditure guidance for 2026 was reiterated at $2.0 billion to $3.0 billion. [Source: Seeking Alpha / Title: Texas Instruments expects Q2 2026 revenue of $5B-$5.4B / Date: 2026-04-22] *

### Management Commentary
CEO Haviv Ilan emphasized that the semiconductor market recovery is continuing, with industrial and data center demand leading. He noted that TI is “well positioned with inventory and capacity that allows us to support our customers with competitive lead times through the cycle.” CFO Rafael Lizardi stated that pricing was stable on a like-for-like basis both sequentially and year-over-year, with the potential for positive pricing resets in the second half of 2026 contingent on demand strength. Lizardi also flagged that acquisition charges related to Silicon Labs would recur quarterly at approximately the Q1 run rate (~$17 million) until closing, after which they would step up and then normalize. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

On free cash flow, Lizardi offered an unusually direct forward claim: “Assuming we do not have another false start, I think it is very likely we will easily be at $8 free cash flow per share for 2026.” This compares with FY2025 FCF per share of roughly $4.77 and trailing-twelve-month FCF per share of approximately $4.75 as of Q1 2026. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

### Forward Claim Confidence Weighting
- **Q2 2026 revenue and EPS guidance: High confidence.** TI’s guidance ranges are typically conservative, and the Q1 beat supports visibility.
- **2026 capex of $2B–$3B: High confidence.** Management has repeatedly described 2026 as the end of the elevated capex cycle, and Q1 capex of $676 million annualizes below the midpoint.
- **$8 FCF per share for 2026: Medium confidence.** The math works if revenue grows in line with guidance, gross margins expand toward 60%, and capex declines as planned. However, it assumes no significant demand deceleration in the second half.
- **Positive pricing resets in H2 2026: Low confidence.** Management explicitly conditioned this on demand strength; it is an option, not a base case.
- **Silicon Labs closing in H1 2027: Medium confidence.** Subject to regulatory approvals and shareholder votes; no financing condition, but antitrust review in the current environment introduces uncertainty.

---

## 8. Material Financial Risks & One-Off Items

- **Cyclical demand deceleration:** Industrial and automotive markets are historically cyclical. While current trends are strong, management has noted that the recovery is slower than prior upturns. A macroeconomic deterioration or inventory correction could stall the rebound. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***
- **China exposure and trade policy:** Approximately 20% of revenue comes from China-headquartered customers, down from higher historical levels but still material. Retaliatory tariffs, export controls, or competition from state-subsidized Chinese analog vendors present ongoing risks. TI’s “China-for-China” manufacturing mitigates some, but not all, of this exposure. [Source: AInvest / Title: Texas Instruments Rises on Strong Earnings, but Tariff Risks Loom Large / Date: 2025-04-24] *
- **Automotive weakness in China and Europe:** Automotive grew only mid-single digits in Q1 2026 and was flat sequentially. China automotive demand declined, and European passenger car production has been under sustained pressure. This tempers the bull case for TI’s second-largest end market. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] *** [Source: The Tech Investor / Title: The Week Semis Made History / Date: 2026-04-25] *
- **Gross margin recovery risk:** While gross margin improved to 58% in Q1 2026, it remains well below the 10-year median of ~64%. If factory loadings stall or pricing does not recover, the path back to 60%+ margins could be delayed.
- **Silicon Labs execution and financing:** The $7.5 billion acquisition will increase leverage and integration risk. TI plans to move Silicon Labs’ production in-house, which entails execution risk. Management has guided to $450 million in annual synergies within three years, but realization is unproven. [Source: EE News Europe / Title: TI Silicon Labs acquisition / Date: 2026-02-06] *
- **Restructuring and goodwill:** The $117 million restructuring charge in FY2025 included a non-cash goodwill impairment for custom ASIC products, indicating that certain legacy assets may be structurally impaired. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

## 9. Discrepancies vs. Structured Data Baseline

### Research Data vs. Stage 1 Baseline
Stage 1 baseline was not provided at runtime. This section is deferred. All findings above are derived from primary SEC filings, company earnings releases, and earnings call transcripts dated January 2026 through April 2026.

## 10. Key Financial Assumptions Embedded in Current Valuation

As of early May 2026, analyst consensus price targets cluster around $266, roughly in line with the then-current share price. The valuation setup appears to embed the following financial assumptions:

- **Revenue growth:** The market prices in a sustained cyclical recovery with revenue growth comfortably above mid-single digits through 2026, likely implying full-year 2026 revenue near $20 billion (based on Q1 actuals and Q2 guidance). This assumes industrial demand continues to close the gap to 2022 peaks and data-center growth remains elevated.
- **Gross margin recovery to 60%+:** The current 58% gross margin is expected to expand toward 60% or higher by late 2026 as 300mm loadings improve and incremental fall-through of 75%–85% materializes.
- **Capex moderation:** Investors are assuming 2026 capex comes in at the guided $2 billion–$3 billion and stays contained thereafter, allowing free cash flow to inflect materially.
- **$8+ free cash flow per share in 2026:** This management claim appears to be a load-bearing assumption for bullish valuation cases. Achieving it requires roughly $7.3 billion in FCF for the year (on ~910 million shares), implying a steep second-half ramp from the $1.4 billion generated in Q1.
- **Successful Silicon Labs integration:** The market appears to be treating the $7.5 billion acquisition as value-creating, assuming synergies materialize and TI can reshore manufacturing without major disruptions.
- **Stable tax rate:** The embedded assumption is an effective tax rate in the low-to-mid teens, supported by OBBBA (One Big Beautiful Bill Act) benefits and CHIPS Act credits. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***

If these assumptions fail—if industrial demand stalls in Q3/Q4, if gross margin stalls below 60%, or if capex does not decline as sharply—the valuation framework would need to reset lower.

---

## 11. Bottom-Line Financial Assessment

### Business Model Characterization
Texas Instruments is a vertically integrated, analog-and-embedded semiconductor manufacturer with a capital-intensive, long-cycle business model. Its financial reality is defined by manufacturing leverage (300mm wafer economics), end-market cyclicality (industrial and automotive), and a deliberate strategy to internalize production through multi-billion-dollar U.S. fab investments. The company returns the majority of free cash flow to shareholders via dividends, with buybacks as a secondary, more variable tool. The archetype that emerges is that of a cyclical, high-margin industrial compounder currently exiting a heavy-investment phase.

### Strengths (confidence weighted)
- **Manufacturing scale and 300mm cost advantage: High confidence.** TI’s internal 300mm analog production is a durable differentiator that should yield lower per-unit costs as new fabs ramp. Gross margin fall-through guidance of 75%–85% is industry-leading. [Source: Company Q1 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***
- **Cash generation and shareholder returns: High confidence.** Trailing-twelve-month operating cash flow of $7.8 billion and a 22-year dividend increase streak demonstrate a shareholder-friendly capital allocation model. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] ***
- **End-market diversification with structural growth drivers: Medium confidence.** Industrial automation, automotive electrification, and data-center power/content are long-term growth markets. TI’s broad catalog creates switching costs.
- **Strong balance sheet and investment-grade liquidity: High confidence.** Long-dated debt, undrawn credit facilities, and a 4% weighted average coupon provide financial flexibility. [Source: Company 8-K / Title: TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

### Weaknesses (confidence weighted)
- **Gross margin still far below historical peaks: High confidence.** At 58%, gross margin sits near the bottom of the 10-year range (57.5%–68.8%) and well below the 63.9% median. Depreciation burdens from new fabs will persist even as loadings improve. [Source: GuruFocus / Title: TXN Gross Margin % / Date: 2026-01-16] **
- **Embedded Processing profitability drag: Medium confidence.** LFAB ramp costs disproportionately burden this segment, and operating margins of 16.9% in Q1 2026 are well below Analog’s 41.7%. Until LFAB reaches scale, Embedded will dilute consolidated returns. [Source: Company 10-K / Title: 2025 Annual Report / Date: 2026-03-04] ***
- **Heavy China exposure in a deteriorating trade environment: Medium confidence.** While TI has executed a “China-for-China” strategy, roughly 20% of revenue remains tied to China-headquartered customers in an era of escalating tariffs and local competition. [Source: AInvest / Title: Texas Instruments Rises on Strong Earnings, but Tariff Risks Loom Large / Date: 2025-04-24] *
- **Capital intensity and ROIC dilution: Medium confidence.** The $60 billion U.S. manufacturing commitment has expanded the invested capital base faster than earnings, compressing ROIC from 30%+ peaks to roughly 20%. It will take years of higher utilization to restore peak capital efficiency. [Source: GuruFocus / Title: TXN ROIC % / Date: 2026-01-16] **

### Watch Items
- **Q2 2026 gross margin trajectory:** Whether gross margin can sustain its sequential expansion into the low-to-mid 59% range will signal how much operating leverage the recovery truly delivers.
- **Industrial order sustainability:** Industrial demand grew 30%+ in Q1 2026, but management notes the market is still 15% below 2022 peaks. Watch for any deceleration in Q3 guidance.
- **Automotive China demand:** A further decline would confirm that this region is structurally weakening for foreign chip vendors.
- **Silicon Labs regulatory timeline:** Antitrust reviews in the U.S., EU, and China could delay the expected H1 2027 close and defer synergy realization.
- **2026 second-half pricing:** Management has floated the possibility of positive price resets in H2. If this fails to materialize, margin estimates for 2027 will need to come down.

---

## 12. Data Gaps & Limitations

- **Geographic revenue breakdown for Q1 2026:** No data found for quarterly geographic splits; search scope included Q1 2026 8-K, earnings call transcript, and 10-K. The most recent granular geographic data is from the 2025 annual report. Analytical impact: limits precision in quantifying China revenue exposure as of the most recent quarter.
- **Exact days sales outstanding for Q1 2026:** Not explicitly stated in the Q1 2026 press release or transcript; inferred from FY2025 DSO of 40 days and accounts receivable balance. # News & Narrative Deep Research: Texas Instruments (TXN)

Research conducted: 2026-05-07

## 1. Most Significant News in the Window

The dominant narrative on Texas Instruments has been rewritten over the past 90 days by a trio of interconnected developments: a dramatic first-quarter earnings inflection, the announcement of a major acquisition, and a decisive pivot in capital-spending strategy that signals a free-cash-flow recovery.

**Q1 2026 Earnings Beat and Guidance Surprise (Event: April 22, 2026).** Texas Instruments reported revenue of $4.83 billion, up 19% year-over-year and well above the $4.53 billion consensus. GAAP earnings per share came in at $1.68, up 31% YoY, against estimates centered near $1.27–$1.37. The stock responded with its largest single-day gain since 2000, closing at a then-record and extending to roughly $283 by early May. [Source: CNBC / Title: "Texas Instruments stock soars on Q1 earnings as AI demand jumps" / Date: 2026-04-23] **;** [Source: Company press release / Title: "TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results and shareholder returns" / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

Management guided Q2 2026 revenue to $5.0–$5.4 billion (roughly 8% sequential growth at the midpoint), which CEO Haviv Ilan characterized as “a little bit above seasonal.” Q2 EPS guidance was set at $1.77–$2.05. [Source: Company Q1 2026 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

**Silicon Labs Acquisition (Announced: February 4, 2026).** TI agreed to acquire Silicon Laboratories in an all-cash transaction valued at approximately $7.5 billion ($231 per share), a roughly 69% premium. The deal, TI’s largest since National Semiconductor in 2011, adds roughly 1,200 wireless connectivity products and expands the company’s embedded processing footprint into edge AI and IoT. TI expects approximately $450 million in annual manufacturing and operational synergies within three years and anticipates the deal closing in the first half of 2027, subject to regulatory approvals. Financing will come from cash on hand and debt. [Source: Reuters / Title: "Texas Instruments strikes $7.5 billion deal for Silicon Labs" / Date: 2026-02-04] **;** [Source: The Wall Street Journal / Title: "Texas Instruments to Acquire Silicon Labs in Deal Valued at $7.5 Billion" / Date: 2026-02-04] **;** [Source: CNBC / Title: "Texas Instruments to buy chip designer Silicon Laboratories" / Date: 2026-02-04] **

**Capital Management Reset (Disclosed: February 25, 2026).** At its 2026 Capital Management Call, TI slashed its capital expenditure outlook to $2–$3 billion for 2026 (down from roughly $4.6 billion in 2025), narrowed its inventory target range to 150–250 days, and reiterated a framework targeting more than $8 in free cash flow per share for 2026 as revenue grows and CapEx moderates. [Source: Seeking Alpha / Title: "Texas Instruments expects Q2 2026 revenue of $5B-$5.4B while targeting $2B-$3B 2026 CapEx" / Date: 2026-04-22] *;** [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: "Texas Instruments Slashes 2026 CapEx Outlook, Targets $8+ Free Cash Flow Per Share" / Date: 2026-02-25] **

**CHIPS Act Funding Milestone.** In Q1 2026, TI received $555 million in direct CHIPS Act funding tied to production start-up at its newest 300-millimeter wafer fab in Sherman, Texas. Over the trailing twelve months, TI has recognized $965 million in CHIPS Act incentives. [Source: Company Q1 2026 earnings call / Title: Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript / Date: 2026-04-22] ***

---

## 2. Earnings Call Deep Dive

### Headline Takeaways
The Q1 2026 call delivered a bull case in three parts: (1) broad-based industrial recovery, with industrial revenue up more than 30% YoY and data-center revenue up roughly 90% YoY; (2) stable pricing and early hints of potential price increases in the second half; and (3) operating leverage validated by a 58% gross margin and operating margin near 38%. Management framed the quarter as evidence that years of capacity investment were now enabling share recapture.

### Management Tone & Conviction
Ilan sounded operationally confident but deliberately cautious on demand sustainability. On preparedness, he struck an assertive note: *“We are prepared. If the market wants to grow at the same rate as Q1, we mentioned 19% year over year, we are ready. If it wants to accelerate, we are ready as well.”* [Source: CNBC / Title: "Texas Instruments stock soars on Q1 earnings as AI demand jumps" / Date: 2026-04-23] **

However, when framing the outlook, Ilan repeatedly invoked the ghost of 2025. He described last year’s cyclical uptick as *“a head fake, a false start”* and added, *“I want to see it play out in 2026.”* This hesitancy was not a one-off; he told analysts the company would *“play it quarter by quarter”* rather than extrapolate the first-half surge into the second half. [Source: Seeking Alpha / Title: "Texas Instruments expects Q2 2026 revenue of $5B-$5.4B while targeting $2B-$3B 2026 CapEx" / Date: 2026-04-22] *;** [Source: The Motley Fool / Title: "TXN Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript" / Date: 2026-04-22] *

CFO Rafael Lizardi, meanwhile, expressed high conviction on the free-cash-flow inflection. When asked about the $8 free cash flow per share target, he replied: *“Assuming we do not have another false start, I think it is very likely we will easily be at $8 free cash flow per share for 2026.”* He also reaffirmed incremental gross-margin fall-through guidance of 75%–85%. [Source: The Motley Fool / Title: "TXN Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript" / Date: 2026-04-22] *

### Q&A Pushback & Analyst Skepticism
Two lines of analyst skepticism dominated the Q&A.

**Sustainability and double-ordering.** Bank of America’s Vivek Arya pressed Ilan on whether the 30% industrial growth reflected inventory replenishment, price, share gains, or *“double ordering or hoarding.”* Ilan pushed back directly: *“No, I do not see it that way, at least I do not have the evidence to show that.”* But he conceded that industrial revenue remained 15% below its 2022 peak, implying the current surge could still be catch-up rather than a new plateau. [Source: The Motley Fool / Title: "TXN Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript" / Date: 2026-04-22] *

**Capacity timing and competitive dynamics.** An analyst from Cantor Fitzgerald questioned the $2–$3 billion CapEx plan and when TI would need to add incremental equipment, especially as tier-two foundries raise pricing. Management’s response—emphasizing modular build-outs and stable lead times—avoided giving a precise re-acceleration trigger for equipment spend. [Source: The Motley Fool / Title: "TXN Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript" / Date: 2026-04-22] *

### Forward Commentary & Guidance
Beyond the quantitative Q2 guide, management noted that pricing was *“stable, flat if you will, like-for-like”* sequentially and YoY, but Ilan flagged that analog market pricing had been moving higher recently, creating the possibility of price increases in the second half depending on demand. The automotive segment was described as holding near prior peaks, though China automotive was singled out as a pocket of weakness. Inventory days fell sequentially from 222 to 209, but the company’s updated long-term target of 150–250 days suggests a willingness to carry more inventory than in past cycles. [Source: The Motley Fool / Title: "TXN Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript" / Date: 2026-04-22] *;** [Source: TIKR / Title: "Texas Instruments Stock Is Up 29% in 2026" / Date: 2026-04-20] *

---

## 3. Analyst Coverage & Rating Changes

### Consensus Picture
Analyst views are split between momentum-driven upgrades and holdouts who see the stock as priced for perfection. As of late April 2026, one compilation showed 13 Buy ratings, 2 Outperforms, 17 Holds, and 5 Sell/Underperform ratings, with a mean price target of $223.89—below where the stock was already trading. [Source: TIKR / Title: "Texas Instruments Stock Is Up 29% in 2026" / Date: 2026-04-20] * Another aggregator pegged the consensus price target at $266.35 as of May 7, 2026, though this appears to blend recently raised targets with stale pre-rally estimates. [Source: Public Investing / Title: "Texas Instruments (TXN) Stock Forecast" / Date: 2026-05-07] *

### Specific Rating Actions in the Window
- **Bank of America:** Upgraded to Buy from Neutral on April 23, 2026, raising the price target to $320 from $235. The firm cited TI’s “solid” Q1 report, confidence in an industrial resurgence, data-center build-out exposure, and the ability to leverage three years of U.S. fab capex to gain share in a supply-constrained analog environment. [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: "Texas Instruments upgraded, Avis downgraded: Wall Street's top analyst calls" / Date: 2026-04-23] **
- **Barclays:** Upgraded to Equal Weight from Underweight on April 23, 2026, lifting the target to $250 from $175. [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: "Texas Instruments upgraded, Avis downgraded: Wall Street's top analyst calls" / Date: 2026-04-23] **
- **Wolfe Research:** Maintained Outperform and raised its price target to $315 from $260 following the Q1 beat. [Source: GuruFocus / Title: "Texas Instruments (TXN) Receives Bullish Analyst Upgrades After Positive Earnings Report" / Date: 2026-04-27] *
- **Stifel:** Upgraded to Buy from Hold on April 9, 2026, with a $250 target, citing five tailwinds: structural FCF inflection, the Silicon Labs acquisition, AI data-center growth, the 300mm manufacturing transition, and analog share gains. [Source: TIKR / Title: "Texas Instruments Stock Is Up 29% in 2026" / Date: 2026-04-20] *

---

## 4. Regulatory, Legal & Compliance Developments

The only material regulatory file in the window is the antitrust and foreign-investment review process for the Silicon Labs acquisition. The definitive proxy statement, filed March 13, 2026, confirms that the transaction is subject to Hart-Scott-Rodino (HSR) review in the U.S., with filings intended by March 20, 2026, as well as merger-control clearance in China and other jurisdictions. Because Silicon Labs derives roughly 15% of its sales from China and TI around 20%, State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) approval is a material gating item. U.S. antitrust risk is viewed as limited given the fragmented nature of the IoT semiconductor market. Termination fees are $259 million for Silicon Labs and $499 million for TI. [Source: SEC PREM14A filing / Title: PREM14A Proxy Statement / Date: 2026-03-13] ***;** [Source: Futurum Group / Title: "Texas Instruments Buys Silicon Labs To Fuel Edge AI Scale" / Date: 2026-02-10] *

No other material regulatory or legal developments were identified in the research window.

---

## 5. Corporate Events: M&A, Leadership, Restructuring, Capital Actions

### M&A Activity
On February 4, 2026, TI announced the $7.5 billion all-cash acquisition of Silicon Labs. The deal is expected to close in the first half of 2027. [Source: Reuters / Title: "Texas Instruments strikes $7.5 billion deal for Silicon Labs" / Date: 2026-02-04] **

### Leadership Changes
Senior Vice President Hagop Kozanian plans to retire in August 2026. The announcement was made in late March 2026. [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: "Why Texas Instruments (TXN) Is Up 7.0% After $30 Billion Fab Bet" / Date: 2026-04-07] **

### Restructuring & Workforce Actions
No material new restructuring actions were identified in the 90-day window. A legacy fab shutdown in North Texas is generating limited headcount reductions (20 additional layoffs expected in April 2026), but these appear to be legacy footprint rationalization rather than a broad workforce restructuring. [Source: LinkedIn / Title: "Texas Instruments cuts 163 jobs in North Texas amid $40B expansion" / Date: 2025-10-05] (Note: Event date references late 2025/early 2026; included only for completeness.)

### Capital Return Announcements
TI returned $6 billion to shareholders over the trailing twelve months, including $1.3 billion in dividends and $158 million in share repurchases during Q1 2026. The quarterly dividend remains $1.42 per share. The market appears to be reading the capital-return program as a complement to, rather than a substitute for, growth, especially given the explicit FCF-per-share framework. [Source: Company press release / Title: "TI reports first quarter 2026 financial results and shareholder returns" / Date: 2026-04-22] ***;** [Source: The Motley Fool / Title: "TXN Q1 2026 Earnings Transcript" / Date: 2026-04-22] *

### Material Insider Transactions
CEO Haviv Ilan sold 20,000 shares at a total value of approximately $5.6 million on May 6, 2026, reducing his direct holdings to 204,339 shares. Over the trailing three months, aggregate insider selling totaled roughly $26.5 million. The CEO transaction is an open-market sale and comes amid a 63% year-to-date rally in the stock. [Source: TradingView / Title: "Texas Instruments Chairman, President & CEO Sold Shares Worth Over $5.6m" / Date: 2026-05-06] *;** [Source: GuruFocus / Title: "Texas Instruments (TXN) Receives Bullish Analyst Upgrades After Positive Earnings Report" / Date: 2026-04-27] *

---

## 6. Sentiment & Positioning

### Institutional Sentiment
Institutional ownership is approximately 91% of the float. Selected Q4 2025/Q1 2026 filings showed incremental buying: Cambiar Investors increased its stake by 33.3% to 315,962 shares (about $54.8 million), making TXN its eighth-largest position, while United Asset Strategies added 5,448 shares. [Source: MarketBeat / Title: "Texas Instruments Incorporated $TXN Position Increased by United Asset Strategies Inc" / Date: 2026-04-24] *;** [Source: MarketBeat / Title: "Texas Instruments Incorporated $TXN is Cambiar Investors LLC's 8th Largest Position" / Date: 2026-04-12] * Overall 13F trends in the most recent quarter suggest institutional accumulation, though comprehensive fund-flow data is incomplete.

### Retail & Social Sentiment
Retail sentiment spiked to “extremely bullish” on StockTwits around the Q1 earnings release, with message volume surging. However, this appears to be an earnings-reaction spike rather than a sustained retail-driven narrative. There is no evidence that retail positioning is a primary driver of the stock’s trajectory. Marked explicitly as lower-signal than institutional positioning. [Source: StockTwits / Title: "Texas Instruments’ Retail Sentiment Hits 3-Month High" / Date: 2025-07-23] (Note: Stale reference from 2025; no material retail sentiment data found for the current window.)

### Short Interest
Most recent as of April 2026: short interest stood at 17.77 million shares, representing 1.96% of float with 2.4 days to cover. This is down 11% from the prior period and is generally considered low, suggesting limited bearish conviction in the near term. [Source: ShortSqueeze.com / Title: "TXN - Short Squeeze Stock Short Interest for Texas Instruments" / Date: 2026-Apr] *

### Valuation Narrative
The market is actively debating whether the stock’s rerating is justified. By late April, TI was trading at a trailing P/E near 47x according to GuruFocus, well above its five-year median of roughly 25x. TIKR data showed NTM EV/EBITDA of ~23x and NTM P/E of ~35x, roughly in line with Broadcom and at a slight discount to ASML. Bulls argue the multiple is warranted by a step-change in FCF (from ~$2.6 billion in 2025 to a consensus ~$6.5 billion in 2026) and by TI’s newly articulated exposure to AI data-center power. Bears counter that the stock has run ahead of the evidence, noting that many analyst price targets remain below the spot price and that management itself refuses to endorse a sustained second-half acceleration. [Source: GuruFocus / Title: "Texas Instruments (TXN) Receives Bullish Analyst Upgrades After Positive Earnings Report" / Date: 2026-04-27] *;** [Source: TIKR / Title: "Texas Instruments Stock Is Up 29% in 2026" / Date: 2026-04-20] *

---

## 7. Short-Seller Research & Activist Investor Involvement

No short-seller reports were identified in the research window.

Activist involvement is a legacy overhang rather than an active fight. In May 2024, Elliott Investment Management disclosed a $2.5 billion stake and publicly urged TI to adopt a “dynamic capacity-management strategy” and target at least $9.00 in free cash flow per share in 2026. TI’s February 2026 Capital Management Call—cutting CapEx to $2–$3 billion and reaffirming an $8+ FCF-per-share target—appears to have addressed Elliott’s core demands. No new 13D filings or public letters from Elliott were identified in the 2026 window. [Source: Investopedia / Title: "Activist Investor Calls on Texas Instruments To Boost Free Cash Flow" / Date: 2024-05-28] *;** [Source: Yahoo Finance / Title: "Texas Instruments Slashes 2026 CapEx Outlook, Targets $8+ Free Cash Flow Per Share" / Date: 2026-02-25] **

---

## 8. Narrative Shifts

A clear narrative shift has occurred.

**Prior narrative (late 2024–early 2025):** TI was viewed as a capital-intensive analog cyclical that had deviated from its free-cash-flow-per-share mandate. Years of heavy fab spending (~$4.6 billion in 2025) had compressed cash generation, invited activist pressure, and produced repeated “false starts” in industrial demand. The stock was a show-me story.

**Current narrative (May 2026):** TI is increasingly framed as an AI-infrastructure and industrial-recovery beneficiary with a structurally improving free-cash-flow profile. The data-center end market (up ~90% YoY) has given TI a growth vector that overlaps with the AI build-out, while the Silicon Labs deal reframes the embedded business as a wireless/edge-AI platform. CapEx moderation and CHIPS Act support have shifted the story from cash burn to cash generation.

**Trigger:** The shift was catalyzed by the combination of the Q1 2026 earnings beat on April 22, the above-seasonal Q2 guide, and the February 25 Capital Management Call that formalized the CapEx downshift and FCF framework. The 19% single-day price move and subsequent rally to ~$283 forced a repricing of the cycle.

---

## 9. Narrative-vs-Reality Disconnects

A material disconnect exists between the market’s pricing and management’s own caution.

**Valuation vs. sustainability evidence.** The stock has rerated to a trailing P/E of roughly 47x (GuruFocus) and trades near or above many recently raised analyst price targets. Yet Ilan explicitly refused to extrapolate the Q1 surge into the second half, repeatedly referencing the 2025 “false start” and stating the company would “play it quarter by quarter.” The market appears to be pricing a sustained cyclical acceleration; management is describing a strong but still-proving recovery.

**Insider selling vs. bullish sell-side upgrades.** While analysts were upgrading the stock in late April and early May, insiders were significant net sellers. The CEO’s $5.6 million open-market sale on May 6, and $26.5 million in total insider selling over the prior three months, suggests management does not share the market’s conviction that the current price represents a durable floor.

**Inventory and cycle risk.** TI’s inventory days remain elevated at 209, and the company has widened its long-term target range to 150–250 days. A Jefferies analyst flagged that the elevated inventory target could signal risk of future write-downs if demand cools, challenging the FCF expansion narrative the market is pricing in. [Source: TIKR / Title: "Texas Instruments Stock Is Up 29% in 2026" / Date: 2026-04-20] *

---

## 10. Bottom-Line Narrative Assessment

### Narrative Characterization
Texas Instruments is currently trading as a late-cycle recovery story with an AI-adjacent growth kicker. The dominant market story is that years of heavy manufacturing investment are now paying off via share recapture in a tight analog market, while data-center power demand and a resurgent industrial sector drive revenue above seasonal trends. Free cash flow is expected to inflect sharply as CapEx falls, validating the premium valuation.

### Key Narrative Drivers (confidence weighted)
1. **Industrial and data-center demand inflection** — *High confidence.* Q1 2026 printed >30% industrial and ~90% data-center YoY growth. Broad-based order improvement was confirmed by management across regions and customer sizes.
2. **Free-cash-flow per share recovery via CapEx moderation** — *High confidence.* Management has explicitly guided to $2–$3 billion CapEx in 2026 (down from ~$4.6 billion in 2025) and “highly probable” $8+ FCF per share. The math is straightforward if revenue holds.
3. **Silicon Labs acquisition as strategic pivot to embedded wireless / edge AI** — *Medium confidence.* The strategic logic is sound, but the deal does not close until H1 2027 and carries regulatory risk (especially China SAMR).
4. **Analog pricing power and share recapture** — *Medium confidence.* Management noted stable pricing and potential for second-half increases, but also emphasized competitive pricing and the need for sustained demand to execute on share gains.
5. **CHIPS Act funding and domestic manufacturing advantage** — *Medium confidence.* $965 million in trailing CHIPS incentives supports the Sherman fab ramp, but this is a known quantity rather than a new catalyst.

### Narrative Risks (confidence weighted)
1. **Demand sustainability / another “false start”** — *High confidence.* Management itself is the primary source of this risk, having explicitly compared 2026 to the 2025 head fake. If the second half decelerates, the current valuation leaves little room for error.
2. **Valuation compression from elevated expectations** — *High confidence.* At ~$280+ and P/E near 47x (trailing), the stock is priced for a continuation of the Q1 trajectory. Any guidance moderation in Q2 or Q3 would likely trigger a sharp multiple re-rating.
3. **Regulatory blockage or delay of Silicon Labs deal** — *Medium confidence.* China exposure on both sides creates SAMR uncertainty. The H1 2027 target leaves ample time, but a blocked deal would remove $450 million in expected synergies and embedded growth optionality.
4. **Elevated inventory write-down risk** — *Medium confidence.* Inventory days of 209 and a widened 150–250 day target create downside if orders stall.

### Catalysts to Watch
- **Q2 2026 earnings release:** Expected late July 2026. Will test whether the “above seasonal” guide was conservative or whether industrial/data-center momentum is decelerating.
- **HSR/China regulatory milestones for Silicon Labs:** HSR filing was expected by March 20, 2026; watch for second-request notices or SAMR pre-notification acceptance over the next two quarters.
- **Potential second-half pricing actions:** Management flagged the possibility of analog price increases in H2 2026 if demand remains firm. Any confirmation on subsequent calls would be a margin tailwind.
- **Quarterly dividend ex-dates:** $1.42/share quarterly; next ex-dividend likely in late July or early August 2026 depending on the declaration schedule.

---

## 11. Data Gaps & Limitations

- **Options positioning:** No reliable options-flow or gamma-positioning data was found for the research window. Search scope: general financial data aggregators and options-analytics platforms.
- **Comprehensive Q1 2026 13F institutional flows:** While anecdotal filings (Cambiar, United Asset Strategies) showed buying, a full picture of aggregate institutional buying/selling for the most recent quarter was not available. Search scope: SEC EDGAR aggregator sites, MarketBeat.
- **Complete Q&A transcript depth:** The Motley Fool transcript provided substantial coverage, but the Cantor Fitzgerald question on modular capacity build-outs was truncated before management’s full response. Search scope: earnings call transcript aggregators (Motley Fool, StockTitan, MarketScreener).
- **Current retail-sentiment metrics:** No retail-sentiment data current to April–May 2026 was located. The most relevant social-sentiment reference dates to July 2025. Search scope: StockTwits, Reddit, retail-analytics aggregators.
- **Analyst consensus snapshot staleness:** Several consensus price-target aggregators (e.g., Zacks) displayed figures (~$210 average) that appear to predate the post-earnings rally, creating a fragmented and potentially misleading consensus picture. The report therefore relies on a blend of recently raised targets and aggregated third-party consensus with explicit staleness flags.
